1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note God speaking to Moses concerning the firstborn, at Succoth after the deliverance. Gill notes this was when Moses and the Israelites were at Succoth, shortly after their coming out of Egypt. Matthew Henry notes care is here taken to perpetuate the remembrance of the preservation of Israel's firstborn. The witnesses note the LORD speaking to Moses to give the law of the firstborn, just after the great deliverance; the opening of the command that consecrates the firstborn to God — that God speaks to fix the memorial of the night of the Passover: "the LORD spake unto Moses, saying"; the witnesses note the time and place, that this came at Succoth, "shortly after their coming out of Egypt," while the deliverance was fresh; the witnesses note the occasion, that God now gives the law of the firstborn to "perpetuate the remembrance" of how he had preserved Israel's firstborn when he smote Egypt's; the witnesses note that what follows is no small matter but the consecrating of the firstborn to God, a standing memorial of the redemption; the witnesses note that God, having delivered his people, at once gives them ordinances to keep the memory of it alive in every generation; so that God speaks to consecrate the firstborn after the deliverance; the LORD spake unto Moses, saying. The witnesses note And the LORD spake unto Moses. The witnesses note saying.

The witnesses note God speaking at Succoth, just after the deliverance, to give the law of the firstborn — perpetuating the remembrance of how he preserved Israel's firstborn when he smote Egypt's; God giving ordinances to keep the memory of the redemption alive in every generation.

So the witnesses make the verse the opening of the command consecrating the firstborn. The lesson the expositors draw is that God spoke to fix the memorial of the Passover, consecrating the firstborn, that the redemption be remembered in every generation. The witnesses note the spake unto Moses: "the LORD spake unto Moses" — Gill notes at Succoth; the spake unto Moses; the at Succoth; the after the deliverance; the fresh; the the law. The expositors observe the the firstborn: the law of the firstborn; the the firstborn; the the law; the consecrate; the to God; the the memorial. The witnesses press the perpetuate remembrance: "perpetuate the remembrance" — Henry notes the preservation; the perpetuate remembrance; the Henry; the the preservation; the the firstborn; the kept. The expositors draw the lesson on the the Passover: the memorial of the Passover; the the Passover; the the Passover; the the night; the smote Egypt; the spared. The witnesses observe the no small matter: no small matter but consecration; the no small matter; the no small; the consecration; the the firstborn; the to God. The expositors note the keep the memory: ordinances to keep the memory; the keep the memory; the ordinances; the the memory; the alive; the every generation. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And the LORD spake unto Moses, the saying, and the command. The verse thus records the opening of the command — teaching that God spoke to consecrate the firstborn, that the redemption be remembered in every generation.
2:
"Sanctify unto me all the firstborn, whatsoever openeth the womb among the children of Israel, both of man and of beast: it is mine." The witnesses note God claiming all the firstborn, of man and beast, as his own. Gill notes "sanctify unto me all the firstborn," that is, of males, as the Targum adds, for those, and not females, were sacrificed or redeemed; this sanctification was a setting them apart for sacred use. The witnesses note God commanding that all the firstborn, of man and beast, that opened the womb, be sanctified to him, for they were his; the consecrating of the firstborn as God's peculiar portion, a memorial of the sparing in Egypt — that God lays his claim on the firstborn: "Sanctify unto me all the firstborn, whatsoever openeth the womb... both of man and of beast: it is mine"; the witnesses note the command, "Sanctify unto me all the firstborn" — to set apart the firstborn (of the males, the Targum adds) for sacred use, either to be sacrificed (of clean beasts) or redeemed (of men and unclean beasts); the witnesses note the ground of the claim, "it is mine" — that God claimed the firstborn as his own peculiar right, because he had spared Israel's firstborn when he slew the firstborn of Egypt, so that they were his by redemption; the witnesses note the type the commentators draw, of Christ the firstborn, and of believers as "the church of the firstborn" (Heb. 12:23), sanctified and belonging wholly to God; the witnesses note that what God spares and redeems he claims as his own; so that God sanctifies the firstborn to himself; both of man and of beast: it is mine. The witnesses note Sanctify unto me all the firstborn, whatsoever openeth the womb among the children of Israel. The witnesses note both of man and of beast: it is mine.

The witnesses note God claiming the firstborn, of man and beast, as his own — "it is mine" — because he spared Israel's firstborn when he slew Egypt's, so they were his by redemption; a type of Christ the firstborn and of believers, the church of the firstborn, belonging wholly to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the consecrating of the firstborn as God's portion. The lesson the expositors draw is that God claimed the firstborn as his own by redemption, having spared them in Egypt, a type of Christ and of believers belonging to God. The witnesses note the sanctify the firstborn: "Sanctify unto me all the firstborn" — Gill notes set apart for sacred use; the sanctify the firstborn; the set apart; the sacred use; the the males; the consecrate. The expositors observe the openeth the womb: "whatsoever openeth the womb" — the firstborn; the openeth the womb; the the firstborn; the the first; the of man; the of beast. The witnesses press the it is mine: "it is mine" — God's own right; the it is mine; the God's own; the his right; the his portion; the claimed. The expositors draw the lesson on the spared in Egypt: spared when he slew Egypt's; the spared in Egypt; the spared; the Egypt's; the slew; the by redemption. The expositors note the what he spares he claims: what God spares he claims; the what he spares; the redeemed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the both of man and of beast it is mine, and the consecration. The verse thus records the consecrating of the firstborn — teaching that God claimed them as his own by redemption, a type of Christ and of believers belonging to God.
3:
"And Moses said unto the people, Remember this day, in which ye came out from Egypt, out of the house of bondage; for by strength of hand the LORD brought you out from this place: there shall no leavened bread be eaten." The witnesses note Moses charging the people to remember the day of their deliverance, and to eat no leavened bread. Keil and Delitzsch note Moses said, Remember this day, in which ye came out from Egypt, out of the house of bondage. The witnesses note Moses bidding the people remember the day they came out of Egypt, the house of bondage, brought out by strength of hand, and eat no leavened bread; the charge to keep the memory of the redemption, and to purge out leaven — that Moses presses the duty of remembrance: "Remember this day, in which ye came out from Egypt, out of the house of bondage... there shall no leavened bread be eaten"; the witnesses note the call, "Remember this day" — that the day of their coming out should be kept in lasting memory, never forgotten; the witnesses note the description of their estate, "out of the house of bondage," the cruel slavery from which they were freed; the witnesses note the author of the deliverance, "by strength of hand the LORD brought you out" — that it was no power of their own but God's mighty hand that wrought it, all the glory his; the witnesses note the ordinance joined to the remembrance, "there shall no leavened bread be eaten" — that, in memory of their hasty flight, they should eat unleavened bread; the witnesses note the type the commentators draw, of leaven as sin and corruption, to be purged out by the redeemed (1 Cor. 5:7-8); so that Moses charges the people to remember and to purge leaven; there shall no leavened bread be eaten. The witnesses note Remember this day, in which ye came out from Egypt, out of the house of bondage. The witnesses note for by strength of hand the LORD brought you out from this place: there shall no leavened bread be eaten.

The witnesses note Moses charging the people to remember the day of their deliverance from the house of bondage — brought out by God's strength of hand, all the glory his — and to eat no leavened bread; the type of leaven as sin and corruption, to be purged out by the redeemed.

So the witnesses make the verse the charge to remember the redemption and purge leaven. The lesson the expositors draw is that the people should keep the day of deliverance in lasting memory, owning God's mighty hand, and eat unleavened bread, the type of purging out sin. The witnesses note the remember this day: "Remember this day" — Keil notes lasting memory; the remember this day; the lasting memory; the the day; the never forgotten; the kept. The expositors observe the house of bondage: "out of the house of bondage" — the cruel slavery; the house of bondage; the the slavery; the cruel; the freed; the from Egypt. The witnesses press the strength of hand: "by strength of hand the LORD brought you out" — God's mighty hand; the strength of hand; the God's hand; the mighty; the all the glory; the his. The expositors draw the lesson on the no leavened bread: "no leavened bread be eaten" — unleavened in memory; the no leavened bread; the the hasty flight. The expositors note the purged out: to be purged out by the redeemed; the purged out; the the corruption. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the there shall no leavened bread be eaten, and the charge. The verse thus records the charge to remember — teaching that the people should keep the day in lasting memory, owning God's hand, and purge out leaven.
4:
"This day came ye out in the month Abib." The witnesses note the day of their coming out, in the month Abib. Gill notes this day ye came out of Egypt, on the fifteenth of Nisan, as the Targum of Jonathan. The witnesses note Moses marking that on this day, in the month Abib, they came out of Egypt; the fixing of the very time of the deliverance, that it might be exactly kept — that the date is set down for a lasting memorial: "This day came ye out in the month Abib"; the witnesses note the precision, "This day came ye out" — that the exact day was marked, the day of their going out of Egypt (the fifteenth of the month); the witnesses note the month named, "the month Abib" — the month of green ears (later called Nisan), the first month of the sacred year, made first because of the redemption; the witnesses note the fitness, that the deliverance fell in the spring, the season of new life and harvest beginning, a fit time for the birth of the nation; the witnesses note that God would have the time of his great mercies exactly remembered, the day and the month kept; the witnesses note that the setting of the date roots the feast in real history, no myth but a thing done on a known day; so that the day and month of the deliverance are fixed; This day came ye out in the month Abib. The witnesses note This day came ye out. The witnesses note in the month Abib.

The witnesses note Moses marking the exact day and month of the deliverance — "the month Abib," the first month of the sacred year, made first because of the redemption; God would have the time of his great mercies exactly remembered, the feast rooted in real history.

So the witnesses make the verse the fixing of the time of the deliverance. The lesson the expositors draw is that the exact day and month were marked, that the redemption be exactly remembered, the feast rooted in real history. The witnesses note the this day: "This day came ye out" — Gill notes the fifteenth; the this day; the the fifteenth; the the exact day; the marked; the the going out. The expositors observe the month Abib: "in the month Abib" — the month of green ears; the month Abib; the green ears; the Nisan; the the first month; the the sacred year. The witnesses press the made first: made first by the redemption; the made first; the made first; the the redemption; the the year; the began. The expositors draw the lesson on the the spring: the deliverance in the spring; the the spring; the spring; the new life; the harvest; the the season. The witnesses observe the exactly remembered: the time exactly remembered; the exactly remembered; the exactly; the remembered; the the day; the the month. The expositors note the real history: rooted in real history; the real history; the real; the history; the no myth; the a known day. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the This day came ye out, the in the month Abib, and the date. The verse thus records the fixing of the time — teaching that the exact day and month were marked, that the redemption be exactly remembered.
5:
"And it shall be when the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites... which he sware unto thy fathers to give thee, a land flowing with milk and honey, that thou shalt keep this service in this month." The witnesses note the keeping of the feast appointed for when they should enter the promised land. Gill notes though the whole land was called Canaan, there was one nation in particular so called; the land sworn to the fathers, flowing with milk and honey. The witnesses note God appointing that, when brought into the land sworn to the fathers, flowing with milk and honey, they should keep this service in this month; the feast bound to the inheritance, the ordinance to be kept in the land of promise — that God ties the feast to the coming possession: "when the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites... that thou shalt keep this service in this month"; the witnesses note the promise renewed, "the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites... which he sware unto thy fathers" — that God's bringing them into Canaan, the land sworn to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, was assured, the feast presupposing the certain inheritance; the witnesses note the richness of the land, "flowing with milk and honey," the abundant inheritance promised; the witnesses note the charge, "thou shalt keep this service in this month" — that the feast of unleavened bread should be kept yearly, in this same month, in the land, a perpetual ordinance; the witnesses note that the very command to keep the feast in Canaan was a pledge that they should surely come there; so that the feast is bound to the promised inheritance; that thou shalt keep this service in this month. The witnesses note when the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites... a land flowing with milk and honey. The witnesses note that thou shalt keep this service in this month.

The witnesses note God binding the feast to the coming inheritance — to be kept when they were brought into the land sworn to the fathers, flowing with milk and honey; the very command to keep the feast in Canaan a pledge that they should surely come there.

So the witnesses make the verse the feast bound to the promised land. The lesson the expositors draw is that God appointed the feast to be kept yearly in the land sworn to the fathers, the command itself a pledge of the certain inheritance. The witnesses note the bring thee in: "the LORD shall bring thee into the land" — Gill notes the land of Canaan; the bring thee in; the the land; the of Canaan; the brought; the assured. The expositors observe the sworn to the fathers: "which he sware unto thy fathers" — the covenant oath; the sworn to the fathers; the the oath; the Abraham; the the fathers; the the covenant. The witnesses press the milk and honey: "flowing with milk and honey" — the rich inheritance; the milk and honey; the the inheritance; the rich; the abundant; the promised. The expositors draw the lesson on the keep this service: "keep this service in this month" — yearly in the land; the keep this service; the yearly; the the feast; the in the land; the perpetual. The expositors note the bound to the land: the feast bound to the land; the bound to the land; the the inheritance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that thou shalt keep this service in this month, and the feast. The verse thus records the feast bound to the land — teaching that the feast should be kept in the land sworn to the fathers, the command a pledge of the inheritance.
6:
"Seven days thou shalt eat unleavened bread, and in the seventh day shall be a feast to the LORD." The witnesses note the seven days of unleavened bread, with a feast on the seventh. Gill notes the obligation to eat unleavened bread, and on the seventh day a feast to the LORD, a holy convocation. The witnesses note God appointing seven days of eating unleavened bread, with a feast to the LORD on the seventh; the set length of the feast and its solemn close — that the ordinance is given its measure and its holy day: "Seven days thou shalt eat unleavened bread, and in the seventh day shall be a feast to the LORD"; the witnesses note the term, "Seven days thou shalt eat unleavened bread" — that the whole feast should last seven days, through all which only unleavened bread should be eaten; the witnesses note the solemn close, "in the seventh day shall be a feast to the LORD" — that the last day should be kept as a "feast to the LORD," a holy convocation, a sabbath of rest and sacred assembly to crown the week; the witnesses note that the feast was thus bounded and marked, a stated time of remembrance and worship; the witnesses note the fitness of the number seven, the number of completeness, for a feast of full and finished redemption; the witnesses note that God appoints the times and forms of his worship, that it be done in order; so that the seven-day feast ends in a holy day to the LORD; in the seventh day shall be a feast to the LORD. The witnesses note Seven days thou shalt eat unleavened bread. The witnesses note and in the seventh day shall be a feast to the LORD.

The witnesses note the seven days of unleavened bread crowned by a feast to the LORD on the seventh — a holy convocation and sabbath of rest; the number seven, of completeness, fitting a feast of full and finished redemption; God appointing the times and forms of his worship.

So the witnesses make the verse the measure and holy close of the feast. The lesson the expositors draw is that the feast of unleavened bread should last seven days and end in a holy feast to the LORD, God appointing the times of his worship. The witnesses note the seven days: "Seven days thou shalt eat unleavened bread" — Gill notes the whole feast; the seven days; the the whole feast; the the term; the unleavened; the throughout. The expositors observe the a feast to the LORD: "a feast to the LORD" — a holy convocation; the a feast to the LORD; the a holy convocation; the the seventh day; the sacred assembly; the sabbath. The witnesses press the the solemn close: the solemn close of the week; the the solemn close; the the close; the the week; the crowned; the holy day. The expositors draw the lesson on the bounded and marked: the feast bounded and marked; the bounded and marked; the bounded; the marked; the stated time; the remembrance. The witnesses observe the number seven: seven, the number of completeness; the number seven; the seven; the completeness; the full; the finished. The expositors note the times of worship: God appoints the times of worship; the times of worship; the the times; the worship; the in order; the his forms. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Seven days thou shalt eat unleavened bread, the and in the seventh day shall be a feast to the LORD, and the feast. The verse thus records the measure of the feast — teaching that it should last seven days and end in a holy feast to the LORD.
7:
"Unleavened bread shall be eaten seven days; and there shall no leavened bread be seen with thee, neither shall there be leaven seen with thee in all thy quarters." The witnesses note the strict charge that no leaven be seen anywhere in their borders. Gill notes this is very express, that not only were they to abstain from eating leavened bread, but no leaven was to be seen in all their quarters. The witnesses note God charging that unleavened bread be eaten seven days, and no leaven be seen anywhere in all their borders; the thoroughness of the purging of leaven, the whole house and land to be clear of it — that God presses the complete removal of leaven: "there shall no leavened bread be seen with thee, neither shall there be leaven seen with thee in all thy quarters"; the witnesses note the strictness, "no leavened bread be seen with thee, neither shall there be leaven seen with thee in all thy quarters" — that not only must they not eat leaven, but none must be found or seen anywhere within their borders, the whole land swept clean of it; the witnesses note the thoroughness, that the purging was to be entire, no trace of leaven left, in all their coasts; the witnesses note the type the commentators draw, that as leaven figures sin and corruption, so the redeemed must purge it out wholly, not tolerating the least of it, keeping the feast "with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth" (1 Cor. 5:8); the witnesses note that God requires not a partial but a complete putting away of sin; so that all leaven must be purged from their borders; neither shall there be leaven seen with thee in all thy quarters. The witnesses note Unleavened bread shall be eaten seven days; and there shall no leavened bread be seen with thee. The witnesses note neither shall there be leaven seen with thee in all thy quarters.

The witnesses note the strict charge that no leaven be seen anywhere in their borders — the whole land swept clean of it; the type that, as leaven figures sin and corruption, the redeemed must purge it out wholly, not tolerating the least, God requiring a complete putting away of sin.

So the witnesses make the verse the thorough purging of leaven. The lesson the expositors draw is that no leaven was to be seen in all their borders, the whole land clear of it, a type that the redeemed must purge out sin wholly. The witnesses note the no leaven seen: "no leavened bread be seen with thee" — Gill notes very express; the no leaven seen; the very express; the not eat; the not seen; the strict. The expositors observe the in all thy quarters: "in all thy quarters" — the whole borders; the in all thy quarters; the the whole borders; the everywhere; the the coasts; the the land. The witnesses press the swept clean: the land swept clean of leaven; the swept clean; the swept; the clean; the no trace; the entire. The expositors draw the lesson on the leaven figures sin: leaven figures sin and corruption; the leaven figures sin; the leaven; the sin; the corruption; the the type. The expositors note the complete putting away: a complete putting away of sin; the complete putting away; the not partial. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the neither shall there be leaven seen with thee in all thy quarters, and the purging. The verse thus records the thorough purging of leaven — teaching that no leaven was to be seen in all their borders, a type that the redeemed must purge out sin wholly.
8:
"And thou shalt shew thy son in that day, saying, This is done because of that which the LORD did unto me when I came forth out of Egypt." The witnesses note the charge to teach the son the reason of the feast. Gill notes this is to be shown not to a single child only, but by parents to all their children, sons and daughters, and even unasked, that they may know the reason of eating unleavened bread. The witnesses note the parent charged to show his son that the feast was kept because of what the LORD did when he came out of Egypt; the duty of teaching the children the meaning of the ordinance — that the feast becomes the occasion of instruction: "thou shalt shew thy son in that day, saying, This is done because of that which the LORD did unto me when I came forth out of Egypt"; the witnesses note the duty laid on parents, "thou shalt shew thy son" — that the keeping of the feast should be made the occasion of teaching the children, and that not one son only but all, sons and daughters, even "unasked"; the witnesses note the substance of the teaching, "This is done because of that which the LORD did unto me" — that the parent should tell the child the reason of the feast, the great deliverance God wrought; the witnesses note the personal owning, "did unto me," as though the parent had himself been redeemed, claiming the mercy as his own; the witnesses note the great duty the commentators draw, that the redeemed should diligently teach their children the works of God, that the memory of his mercies be handed down; so that the feast becomes the means of instructing the children; This is done because of that which the LORD did unto me when I came forth out of Egypt. The witnesses note thou shalt shew thy son in that day. The witnesses note This is done because of that which the LORD did unto me when I came forth out of Egypt.

The witnesses note the duty laid on parents to teach the children the meaning of the feast — the great deliverance God wrought, owned as "done unto me" as if the parent himself were redeemed; the redeemed should diligently teach their children the works of God, that his mercies be handed down.

So the witnesses make the verse the duty of teaching the children. The lesson the expositors draw is that the feast should be the occasion of instructing the children in the deliverance God wrought, handing down the memory of his mercies. The witnesses note the shew thy son: "thou shalt shew thy son" — Gill notes all the children; the shew thy son; the all the children; the sons; the daughters; the even unasked. The expositors observe the the occasion: the feast the occasion of teaching; the the occasion; the the feast; the teaching; the the children; the the meaning. The witnesses press the the LORD did: "that which the LORD did unto me" — the deliverance; the the LORD did; the the deliverance; the the great work; the God wrought; the the reason. The expositors draw the lesson on the unto me: "did unto me" — owned as his own; the unto me; the as his own; the owned; the redeemed; the personal. The expositors note the handed down: the mercies handed down; the handed down; the the memory. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the This is done because of that which the LORD did unto me when I came forth out of Egypt, and the teaching. The verse thus records the duty of teaching the children — teaching that the feast should instruct them in the deliverance God wrought, handing down his mercies.
9:
"And it shall be for a sign unto thee upon thine hand, and for a memorial between thine eyes, that the LORD's law may be in thy mouth: for with a strong hand hath the LORD brought thee out of Egypt." The witnesses note the feast to be a sign on the hand and a memorial between the eyes. Gill notes these are the words of an Israelitish parent to his son, telling him that this feast would serve to refresh his memory with what God did for his people of old. The witnesses note the feast appointed to be a sign on the hand and a memorial between the eyes, that the LORD's law might be in the mouth, since with a strong hand he brought them out; the keeping of the redemption ever in mind and action and speech — that the deliverance should govern hand, eye, and tongue: "it shall be for a sign unto thee upon thine hand, and for a memorial between thine eyes, that the LORD's law may be in thy mouth"; the witnesses note the figures, "a sign... upon thine hand, and... a memorial between thine eyes" — that the redemption should be kept ever in remembrance, as a thing bound on the hand (governing the actions) and set before the eyes (ever in view), and "in thy mouth" (often spoken of); the witnesses note that the Jews took these words literally for the phylacteries (the frontlets bound on hand and brow), but the commentators rather take the sense as a constant, heart-deep remembrance that should rule all of life; the witnesses note the ground, "for with a strong hand hath the LORD brought thee out of Egypt" — that the greatness of the deliverance, wrought by God's strong hand, deserved to be so constantly remembered; the witnesses note that God's great mercies should govern our hands, our eyes, and our tongues; so that the redemption is to be kept ever in hand, eye, and mouth; for with a strong hand hath the LORD brought thee out of Egypt. The witnesses note for a sign unto thee upon thine hand, and for a memorial between thine eyes, that the LORD's law may be in thy mouth. The witnesses note for with a strong hand hath the LORD brought thee out of Egypt.

The witnesses note the redemption to be kept ever in mind and action and speech — a sign on the hand, a memorial between the eyes, the LORD's law in the mouth; the Jews took this literally for the phylacteries, but the commentators rather a constant, heart-deep remembrance ruling all of life.

So the witnesses make the verse the redemption kept ever in hand, eye, and mouth. The lesson the expositors draw is that the great deliverance should be so constantly remembered as to govern the actions, the sight, and the speech, a heart-deep remembrance. The witnesses note the sign on the hand: "a sign... upon thine hand" — governing the actions; the sign on the hand; the the actions; the bound; the the hand; the the deeds. The expositors observe the memorial between the eyes: "a memorial between thine eyes" — ever in view; the memorial between the eyes; the ever in view; the before the eyes; the the sight; the kept. The witnesses press the in thy mouth: "the LORD's law... in thy mouth" — often spoken; the in thy mouth; the the tongue. The expositors note the strong hand: "with a strong hand... brought thee out" — the great deliverance; the strong hand; the the deliverance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for with a strong hand hath the LORD brought thee out of Egypt, and the remembrance. The verse thus records the redemption kept ever in mind — teaching that the deliverance should govern the actions, the sight, and the speech.
10:
"Thou shalt therefore keep this ordinance in his season from year to year." The witnesses note the charge to keep the ordinance yearly at its appointed season. Keil and Delitzsch note this ordinance the Israelites were to keep at its appointed time, from year to year. The witnesses note the people charged to keep this ordinance in its season from year to year; the perpetual and stated observance of the feast appointed — that God binds the feast as a yearly ordinance: "Thou shalt therefore keep this ordinance in his season from year to year"; the witnesses note the duty, "keep this ordinance" — not the ordinance of the phylacteries (as the Targum wrongly took it), but "the ordinance of unleavened bread," the feast just commanded; the witnesses note the time, "in his season" — that it should be kept "at its appointed time," in the right month and days, not at men's pleasure but as God fixed; the witnesses note the perpetuity, "from year to year" — that the feast should be a standing, yearly observance, kept by every generation, a lasting memorial of the redemption; the witnesses note that God appoints set and recurring times of remembrance, that his mercies be not forgotten; the witnesses note the order and constancy of God's worship, kept in its season; so that the feast is to be kept yearly at its set time; from year to year. The witnesses note Thou shalt therefore keep this ordinance in his season. The witnesses note from year to year.

The witnesses note the feast bound as a yearly ordinance, kept at its appointed season from year to year — a standing memorial of the redemption for every generation; God appointing set and recurring times of remembrance, that his mercies be not forgotten.

So the witnesses make the verse the perpetual yearly observance of the feast. The lesson the expositors draw is that the ordinance of unleavened bread should be kept at its appointed time, year by year, a lasting memorial for every generation. The witnesses note the keep this ordinance: "keep this ordinance" — Gill notes of unleavened bread; the keep this ordinance; the of unleavened bread; the the feast; the the ordinance; the not phylacteries. The expositors observe the in his season: "in his season" — at the appointed time; the in his season; the the appointed time; the the right month; the not men's pleasure; the as God fixed. The witnesses press the from year to year: "from year to year" — perpetual; the from year to year; the perpetual; the yearly; the standing; the every generation. The expositors draw the lesson on the a memorial: a lasting memorial; the a memorial; the lasting; the the redemption; the kept; the remembered. The witnesses observe the set times: God appoints set times; the set times; the set; the times; the of remembrance; the recurring. The expositors note the order and constancy: the order and constancy of worship; the order and constancy; the order; the constancy; the worship; the in season. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Thou shalt therefore keep this ordinance in his season, the from year to year, and the yearly observance. The verse thus records the perpetual observance — teaching that the feast should be kept at its appointed time, year by year, a lasting memorial.
11:
"And it shall be when the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites, as he sware unto thee and to thy fathers, and shall give it thee," The witnesses note the law of the firstborn appointed for when they should be brought into the land. Keil and Delitzsch note that in these verses Moses communicated to the people the law concerning the firstborn, for when the LORD should bring them into the land sworn to their fathers. The witnesses note God appointing that, when brought into the land of the Canaanites, as he sware to them and their fathers, and gave it them, the law of the firstborn should be kept; the binding of the law of the firstborn to the coming inheritance — that God ties this ordinance also to the possession of the land: "when the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites, as he sware unto thee and to thy fathers, and shall give it thee"; the witnesses note the renewed assurance, "the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites" — that God would surely bring them into Canaan (put for all the nations of the land); the witnesses note the ground, "as he sware unto thee and to thy fathers" — the covenant oath to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and to the people, a sure foundation; the witnesses note the gift, "and shall give it thee" — that the land should be their inheritance by God's free gift, not their own getting; the witnesses note that the law of the firstborn, like the feast, presupposes the certain entering of the land, the command a pledge of the promise; the witnesses note God's faithfulness to his sworn covenant; so that the law of the firstborn is bound to the coming inheritance; as he sware unto thee and to thy fathers, and shall give it thee. The witnesses note when the LORD shall bring thee into the land of the Canaanites, as he sware unto thee and to thy fathers. The witnesses note and shall give it thee.

The witnesses note God binding the law of the firstborn to the coming inheritance — to be kept when brought into the land sworn to the fathers and given by God's free gift; the command itself presupposing the certain entering of the land, a pledge of the covenant promise.

So the witnesses make the verse the law of the firstborn bound to the land. The lesson the expositors draw is that the law of the firstborn was appointed for the land sworn to the fathers and given by God's gift, the command a pledge of the certain inheritance. The witnesses note the bring thee in: "the LORD shall bring thee into the land" — Keil notes the law of the firstborn; the bring thee in; the the land; the of Canaan; the brought; the assured. The expositors observe the as he sware: "as he sware unto thee and to thy fathers" — the covenant oath; the as he sware; the the oath; the the fathers; the Abraham; the the covenant. The witnesses press the shall give it: "and shall give it thee" — by free gift; the shall give it; the free gift; the not their own; the the inheritance; the God's. The expositors draw the lesson on the presupposes the land: presupposes the entering of the land; the presupposes the land; the presupposes; the the entering; the the command; the the pledge. The expositors note the faithfulness: God's faithfulness to his oath; the faithfulness; the the covenant. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and shall give it thee, and the law of the firstborn. The verse thus records the law bound to the land — teaching that it was appointed for the land sworn to the fathers, the command a pledge of the inheritance.
12:
"That thou shalt set apart unto the LORD all that openeth the matrix, and every firstling that cometh of a beast which thou hast; the males shall be the LORD's." The witnesses note the firstborn of man and beast to be set apart to the LORD. Gill notes "set apart" explains the word "sanctify," showing it signifies separating such to the use and service of God, causing it to pass from a man's own power to be the LORD's. The witnesses note God commanding that all that opened the womb, and every firstling of beast, the males, be set apart to the LORD; the actual consecrating of the firstborn as the LORD's portion — that the firstborn is to be handed over to God: "thou shalt set apart unto the LORD all that openeth the matrix... the males shall be the LORD's"; the witnesses note the act, "set apart unto the LORD" — that the firstborn should be separated from common use and given over wholly "to the use and service of God," passing "from a man's own power" to be the LORD's; the witnesses note the breadth, "all that openeth the matrix, and every firstling... of a beast" — that both the firstborn of men and the firstlings of beasts were claimed; the witnesses note the limit, "the males shall be the LORD's" — that it was the firstborn males that were thus God's, in memory of the firstborn males spared in Egypt; the witnesses note that what God claims as his own must be actually given over to him, not kept back; the witnesses note that the redeemed and their increase belong to God, to be devoted to his service; so that the firstborn males are set apart to the LORD; the males shall be the LORD's. The witnesses note thou shalt set apart unto the LORD all that openeth the matrix. The witnesses note the males shall be the LORD's.

The witnesses note the firstborn of man and beast set apart to the LORD — separated from common use and given wholly to God, passing from a man's own power to be the LORD's; what God claims as his own must be actually given over, the redeemed and their increase belonging to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the actual consecrating of the firstborn. The lesson the expositors draw is that the firstborn males of man and beast were to be set apart and given wholly to God, what he claims actually handed over. The witnesses note the set apart: "set apart unto the LORD" — Gill notes separated to God's use; the set apart; the separated; the God's use; the from common; the given over. The expositors observe the openeth the matrix: "all that openeth the matrix" — the firstborn; the openeth the matrix; the the firstborn; the the first; the of men; the the womb. The witnesses press the every firstling: "every firstling... of a beast" — the firstlings of beasts; the every firstling; the the firstlings; the of beasts; the claimed; the both. The expositors draw the lesson on the males the LORD's: "the males shall be the LORD's" — the firstborn males; the males the LORD's; the the males; the the firstborn; the God's; the spared in Egypt. The expositors note the belong to God: the redeemed and increase belong to God; the belong to God; the devoted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the males shall be the LORD's, and the consecration. The verse thus records the consecrating of the firstborn — teaching that the firstborn males were to be set apart and given wholly to God.
13:
"And every firstling of an ass thou shalt redeem with a lamb; and if thou wilt not redeem it, then thou shalt break his neck: and all the firstborn of man among thy children shalt thou redeem." The witnesses note the firstling of the ass redeemed with a lamb, and the firstborn of man redeemed. Gill notes every firstling of an ass was to be redeemed with a lamb, which was given to the priest for it. The witnesses note God commanding that the firstling of an ass be redeemed with a lamb, or its neck broken, and all the firstborn of man redeemed; the law of redemption for the unclean and for man, a type of redemption by the Lamb — that God appoints a ransom for what could not be offered: "every firstling of an ass thou shalt redeem with a lamb... and all the firstborn of man among thy children shalt thou redeem"; the witnesses note the case of the ass, "every firstling of an ass thou shalt redeem with a lamb" — that the ass, being an unclean beast, could not be offered to God, but must be "redeemed with a lamb" (given to the priest in its stead), or else its "neck broken," it being unfit for the LORD's altar; the witnesses note the case of man, "all the firstborn of man among thy children shalt thou redeem" — that the firstborn sons, being God's, must yet be redeemed with a price (afterward fixed at five shekels), not sacrificed; the witnesses note the rich type the commentators draw, that the unclean ass redeemed by the lamb figures the sinner, unfit for God, redeemed only by Christ the Lamb, who died in his stead; the witnesses note the great gospel truth, that the redeemed are bought with a price, ransomed by the Lamb of God; so that the firstling and the firstborn are redeemed by a ransom; all the firstborn of man among thy children shalt thou redeem. The witnesses note every firstling of an ass thou shalt redeem with a lamb. The witnesses note and all the firstborn of man among thy children shalt thou redeem.

The witnesses note the firstling of the unclean ass redeemed with a lamb (or its neck broken), and the firstborn of man redeemed with a price — a rich type, the unclean ass figuring the sinner, unfit for God, redeemed only by Christ the Lamb who died in his stead.

So the witnesses make the verse the law of redemption, a type of the Lamb. The lesson the expositors draw is that the unclean firstling and the firstborn of man were redeemed by a ransom, a type of the sinner redeemed only by Christ the Lamb. The witnesses note the firstling of an ass: "every firstling of an ass" — Gill notes redeemed with a lamb; the firstling of an ass; the unclean; the not offered. The expositors observe the break his neck: "thou shalt break his neck" — if not redeemed; the break his neck; the unfit; the the altar. The witnesses press the firstborn of man: "all the firstborn of man... shalt thou redeem" — with a price; the firstborn of man; the the sons; the not sacrificed. The expositors draw the lesson on the the ass a type: the unclean ass a type of the sinner; the the ass a type; the the sinner; the unfit. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and all the firstborn of man among thy children shalt thou redeem, and the redemption. The verse thus records the law of redemption — teaching that the unclean firstling and the firstborn of man were redeemed by a ransom, a type of the sinner redeemed by Christ the Lamb.
14:
"And it shall be when thy son asketh thee in time to come, saying, What is this? that thou shalt say unto him, By strength of hand the LORD brought us out from Egypt, from the house of bondage:" The witnesses note the answer to be given the son who asks the meaning of the ordinance. Gill notes when the son asks in time to come, the day following such a separation or redemption of the firstborn, or at any later time. The witnesses note God appointing that, when the son should ask what this meant, the father should answer that by strength of hand the LORD brought them out of Egypt, the house of bondage; the catechizing of the children by their own questions — that the ordinance is to be explained to the inquiring child: "when thy son asketh thee in time to come, saying, What is this? that thou shalt say unto him, By strength of hand the LORD brought us out from Egypt"; the witnesses note the occasion, "when thy son asketh thee... What is this?" — that the child, seeing the strange rites of the firstborn, would naturally ask their meaning, and so give the parent occasion to teach; the witnesses note the answer appointed, "By strength of hand the LORD brought us out from Egypt, from the house of bondage" — that the father should point the child to the great deliverance, wrought by God's mighty hand, as the reason of all; the witnesses note the wisdom of God in ordaining rites that should provoke the children's questions and so lead to instruction; the witnesses note the duty of parents to be ready to give a reason of their worship to their children; so that the son's question is the occasion of teaching the deliverance; By strength of hand the LORD brought us out from Egypt, from the house of bondage. The witnesses note when thy son asketh thee in time to come, saying, What is this? The witnesses note By strength of hand the LORD brought us out from Egypt, from the house of bondage.

The witnesses note the child's question — "What is this?" — made the occasion of teaching the great deliverance, "By strength of hand the LORD brought us out"; the wisdom of God in ordaining rites that provoke the children's questions and lead to instruction.

So the witnesses make the verse the catechizing of the children by their questions. The lesson the expositors draw is that the son's question gave the parent occasion to point the child to the deliverance wrought by God's strong hand, the rite leading to instruction. The witnesses note the asketh thee: "when thy son asketh thee... What is this?" — Gill notes the strange rites; the asketh thee; the the strange rites; the the meaning; the the child; the asks. The expositors observe the occasion to teach: gives occasion to teach; the occasion to teach; the occasion; the to teach; the the parent; the the child. The witnesses press the strength of hand: "By strength of hand the LORD brought us out" — the deliverance; the strength of hand; the the deliverance; the God's hand; the mighty; the the reason. The expositors draw the lesson on the house of bondage: "from the house of bondage" — the slavery; the house of bondage; the the slavery; the freed; the from Egypt; the the bondage. The expositors note the give a reason: parents to give a reason; the give a reason; the their worship. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the By strength of hand the LORD brought us out from Egypt from the house of bondage, and the catechizing. The verse thus records the catechizing of the children — teaching that the son's question gave occasion to point the child to the deliverance.
15:
"And it came to pass, when Pharaoh would hardly let us go, that the LORD slew all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, both the firstborn of man, and the firstborn of beast: therefore I sacrifice to the LORD all that openeth the matrix, being males; but all the firstborn of my children I redeem." The witnesses note the answer continued, the reason of the firstborn law in the slaying of Egypt's firstborn. Gill notes when Pharaoh showed great reluctance to let them go, with difficulty prevailed upon, the LORD slew all the firstborn of Egypt. The witnesses note the father to tell how, when Pharaoh would hardly let them go, the LORD slew all Egypt's firstborn, and so he sacrifices or redeems the firstborn; the ground of the law of the firstborn in the night of the smiting — that the rite is traced to the great deliverance: "when Pharaoh would hardly let us go, that the LORD slew all the firstborn in the land of Egypt... therefore I sacrifice to the LORD all that openeth the matrix... but all the firstborn of my children I redeem"; the witnesses note the occasion recalled, "when Pharaoh would hardly let us go" — Pharaoh's stubborn refusal, that the LORD "slew all the firstborn in the land of Egypt, both of man and beast"; the witnesses note the ground of the law, "therefore I sacrifice to the LORD all that openeth the matrix... but all the firstborn of my children I redeem" — that, because God spared Israel's firstborn and slew Egypt's, the firstborn of Israel, both clean beasts (sacrificed) and men and unclean beasts (redeemed), were the LORD's; the witnesses note that the law of the firstborn was a standing memorial of that night, owning God's right over the spared; the witnesses note that the redeemed should ever own that they are the LORD's by the right of his sparing mercy; so that the firstborn law memorializes the slaying and the sparing; but all the firstborn of my children I redeem. The witnesses note when Pharaoh would hardly let us go, that the LORD slew all the firstborn in the land of Egypt. The witnesses note therefore I sacrifice to the LORD all that openeth the matrix... but all the firstborn of my children I redeem.

The witnesses note the law of the firstborn traced to the night of the smiting — when the LORD slew Egypt's firstborn but spared Israel's — so the firstborn are the LORD's, sacrificed or redeemed; a standing memorial that the redeemed are God's by the right of his sparing mercy.

So the witnesses make the verse the ground of the firstborn law in the deliverance. The lesson the expositors draw is that, because God slew Egypt's firstborn and spared Israel's, the firstborn are the LORD's, sacrificed or redeemed, a memorial of his sparing mercy. The witnesses note the hardly let us go: "when Pharaoh would hardly let us go" — Gill notes great reluctance; the hardly let us go; the stubborn. The expositors observe the slew the firstborn: "the LORD slew all the firstborn" — of man and beast; the slew the firstborn; the smitten. The witnesses press the therefore I sacrifice: "therefore I sacrifice to the LORD" — the clean beasts; the therefore I sacrifice; the the firstlings. The expositors note the by sparing mercy: the redeemed God's by sparing mercy; the by sparing mercy; the his right. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the therefore I sacrifice to the LORD all that openeth the matrix but all the firstborn of my children I redeem, and the memorial. The verse thus records the ground of the firstborn law — teaching that, because God slew Egypt's firstborn and spared Israel's, the firstborn are the LORD's.
16:
"And it shall be for a token upon thine hand, and for frontlets between thine eyes: for by strength of hand the LORD brought us forth out of Egypt." The witnesses note the redemption to be a token on the hand and frontlets between the eyes. Gill notes these laws concerning the firstlings and the redemption of the firstborn would be a token and memorial, as the feast of unleavened bread before. The witnesses note that these laws of the firstborn should be a token upon the hand and frontlets between the eyes, since by strength of hand the LORD brought them out; the keeping of the redemption ever before them in act and thought — that, as with the feast, so the firstborn law is a constant memorial: "it shall be for a token upon thine hand, and for frontlets between thine eyes: for by strength of hand the LORD brought us forth out of Egypt"; the witnesses note the figure repeated (as v.9), "a token upon thine hand, and... frontlets between thine eyes" — that the redemption should be as constantly remembered and as plainly owned as a thing bound on the hand and worn before the eyes, governing the life; the witnesses note that the Jews again took this for the literal phylacteries, but the commentators rather a heart-deep, life-ruling remembrance; the witnesses note the ground once more, "for by strength of hand the LORD brought us forth out of Egypt" — the greatness of the deliverance the reason for so constant a memory; the witnesses note that the great works of God deserve to be kept ever in mind and to shape the whole of life; so that the redemption is to be a constant token before them; for by strength of hand the LORD brought us forth out of Egypt. The witnesses note it shall be for a token upon thine hand, and for frontlets between thine eyes. The witnesses note for by strength of hand the LORD brought us forth out of Egypt.

The witnesses note the law of the firstborn, like the feast, to be a token on the hand and frontlets between the eyes — the redemption ever remembered and owned, governing the life; the great works of God deserving to be kept ever in mind and to shape the whole of life.

So the witnesses make the verse the redemption kept as a constant token. The lesson the expositors draw is that the firstborn law, like the feast, should keep the redemption ever before them in act and thought, the great deliverance ruling the life. The witnesses note the token on the hand: "a token upon thine hand" — Gill notes a memorial; the token on the hand; the a memorial; the the hand; the the act; the bound. The expositors observe the frontlets between the eyes: "frontlets between thine eyes" — before the eyes; the frontlets between the eyes; the before the eyes; the worn; the the sight; the kept. The witnesses press the the phylacteries: the Jews took it for phylacteries; the the phylacteries; the the Jews; the literally; the heart-deep; the rather. The expositors draw the lesson on the strength of hand: "by strength of hand the LORD brought us forth" — the deliverance; the strength of hand; the the deliverance; the God's hand; the the greatness; the the reason. The expositors note the great works remembered: God's great works kept in mind; the great works remembered; the shape life. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for by strength of hand the LORD brought us forth out of Egypt, and the token. The verse thus records the redemption kept as a constant token — teaching that the firstborn law should keep the deliverance ever before them, ruling the life.
17:
"And it came to pass, when Pharaoh had let the people go, that God led them not through the way of the land of the Philistines, although that was near; for God said, Lest peradventure the people repent when they see war, and they return to Egypt:" The witnesses note God leading the people not the near way of the Philistines, lest they meet war and turn back. Matthew Henry notes here is the choice God made of their way, leading them not the nearest but the safest. The witnesses note God leading Israel not through the near Philistine way, lest they see war and repent and return to Egypt; the wise and tender leading of God, choosing the longer but safer way — that God spares his weak people the trial of war too soon: "God led them not through the way of the land of the Philistines, although that was near; for God said, Lest... the people repent when they see war, and they return to Egypt"; the witnesses note the choice, "God led them not through the way of the land of the Philistines, although that was near" — that the short road by the Philistine country was avoided, though it was the nearest to Canaan; the witnesses note the reason, "Lest peradventure the people repent when they see war, and they return to Egypt" — that God knew his people, newly freed and faint-hearted, were not yet able to face the warlike Philistines, and might, at the sight of war, lose heart and turn back to bondage; the witnesses note the tender wisdom of God, who leads his people not by the nearest but by the way they are able to bear, sparing them trials too great for their faith; the witnesses note that God fits the way to the strength of his people, dealing gently with the weak; so that God leads them the safer way, sparing them war too soon; and they return to Egypt. The witnesses note God led them not through the way of the land of the Philistines, although that was near. The witnesses note Lest peradventure the people repent when they see war, and they return to Egypt.

The witnesses note God leading Israel not the near Philistine way but the longer safer road — lest the newly-freed, faint-hearted people see war and turn back to Egypt; the tender wisdom of God, who leads his people by the way they are able to bear, sparing them trials too great for their faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the wise and tender leading of God. The lesson the expositors draw is that God led Israel not the nearest but the safest way, sparing the weak people the sight of war lest they turn back, fitting the way to their strength. The witnesses note the not the Philistine way: "led them not through the way of the Philistines" — Henry notes not the nearest; the not the Philistine way; the not the nearest; the the near road; the avoided; the to Canaan. The expositors observe the lest they see war: "Lest... they see war" — the trial of battle; the lest they see war; the the Philistines. The witnesses press the repent and return: "repent... and return to Egypt" — turn back to bondage; the repent and return; the lose heart. The expositors note the fits the way: God fits the way to their strength; the fits the way; the gently. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Lest peradventure the people repent when they see war and they return to Egypt, and the leading. The verse thus records the wise leading of God — teaching that he led them the safer way, sparing the weak people the sight of war lest they turn back.
18:
"But God led the people about, through the way of the wilderness of the Red sea: and the children of Israel went up harnessed out of the land of Egypt." The witnesses note God leading them about by the wilderness of the Red Sea, the people going up harnessed. Gill notes instead of going toward Gaza and the Mediterranean, the Lord directed them to turn off toward the wilderness of the Red Sea. The witnesses note God leading the people about by the way of the wilderness of the Red Sea, and the children of Israel going up harnessed; the roundabout but appointed way, the people marching out in order — that God leads them the circuitous way that served his purpose: "God led the people about, through the way of the wilderness of the Red sea: and the children of Israel went up harnessed out of the land of Egypt"; the witnesses note the way, "God led the people about, through the way of the wilderness of the Red sea" — that, instead of the short road, God led them by a roundabout way toward the Red Sea, which (though it seemed to entangle them) served God's purpose, both to spare them war and to make a way for his great wonder at the sea; the witnesses note the manner of their going, "the children of Israel went up harnessed" (or "in orderly array," or "armed") out of Egypt — that they marched out not as fleeing fugitives in confusion, but in order, ranked and equipped, like an army; the witnesses note that God brought them out with dignity and order, his hosts (7:4) marching forth; the witnesses note that God's roundabout ways for his people are always wise, leading them where his glory and their good require; so that God leads them about, his ordered hosts going up; the children of Israel went up harnessed out of the land of Egypt. The witnesses note God led the people about, through the way of the wilderness of the Red sea. The witnesses note and the children of Israel went up harnessed out of the land of Egypt.

The witnesses note God leading the people the roundabout way by the wilderness of the Red Sea — which served his purpose, both to spare them war and to make a way for his wonder at the sea — and Israel going up harnessed, in orderly array like an army; God's roundabout ways for his people always wise.

So the witnesses make the verse the roundabout but appointed way and the ordered march. The lesson the expositors draw is that God led them about by the Red Sea, the circuitous way serving his purpose, and Israel went up in orderly array, God's roundabout ways always wise. The witnesses note the led about: "God led the people about" — Gill notes toward the Red Sea; the led about; the toward the Red Sea; the the roundabout; the the wilderness; the turned off. The expositors observe the served his purpose: the way served God's purpose; the served his purpose; the served; the his purpose; the the wonder; the at the sea. The witnesses press the went up harnessed: "went up harnessed" — in orderly array; the went up harnessed; the orderly array; the armed; the ranked; the equipped. The expositors draw the lesson on the not fugitives: not as fleeing fugitives; the not fugitives; the in order. The expositors note the roundabout ways wise: God's roundabout ways wise; the roundabout ways wise; the his glory. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and the children of Israel went up harnessed out of the land of Egypt, and the way. The verse thus records the roundabout way and the ordered march — teaching that God led them about by the Red Sea, the circuitous way serving his purpose.
19:
"And Moses took the bones of Joseph with him: for he had straitly sworn the children of Israel, saying, God will surely visit you; and ye shall carry up my bones away hence with you." The witnesses note Moses taking Joseph's bones, as Joseph had charged the children of Israel by oath. Gill notes Moses took the bones of Joseph with him, his remains well called bones, since at such a distance from his death the flesh must be gone. The witnesses note Moses taking Joseph's bones with him, for Joseph had strictly sworn Israel that, when God visited them, they should carry up his bones; the keeping of Joseph's dying charge, the faith of the patriarch honoured — that the oath made long before is now performed: "Moses took the bones of Joseph with him: for he had straitly sworn the children of Israel, saying, God will surely visit you; and ye shall carry up my bones"; the witnesses note the care of Moses, that amid all the haste and tumult of the going out, he "took the bones of Joseph with him," not forgetting the sacred charge; the witnesses note the oath recalled, that Joseph "had straitly sworn the children of Israel" (Gen. 50:25), binding them that when "God will surely visit you" they should carry up his bones to Canaan; the witnesses note the faith of Joseph in that dying charge, that he believed God's promise to bring his seed into the land, and would have his bones rest there, a witness to the coming deliverance (Heb. 11:22); the witnesses note that the oath was now honoured, the faith of the patriarch confirmed by the event; so that Moses carries up Joseph's bones, the oath kept; ye shall carry up my bones away hence with you. The witnesses note Moses took the bones of Joseph with him: for he had straitly sworn the children of Israel. The witnesses note God will surely visit you; and ye shall carry up my bones away hence with you.

The witnesses note Moses, amid all the haste, taking Joseph's bones — keeping the oath Joseph had bound on Israel ("God will surely visit you") — honouring the faith of the patriarch who believed God's promise and would have his bones rest in the promised land (Hebrews 11:22).

So the witnesses make the verse the keeping of Joseph's dying charge. The lesson the expositors draw is that Moses honoured the oath, carrying up Joseph's bones, the faith of the patriarch who believed God would bring his seed into the land confirmed by the event. The witnesses note the took the bones: "Moses took the bones of Joseph" — Gill notes amid the haste; the took the bones; the amid the haste; the not forgotten; the the charge; the carried. The expositors observe the straitly sworn: "he had straitly sworn the children of Israel" — the oath; the straitly sworn; the the oath; the bound; the Israel; the Genesis. The witnesses press the God will visit: "God will surely visit you" — the promise believed; the God will visit; the the promise; the believed; the the deliverance; the faith. The expositors draw the lesson on the carry up my bones: "ye shall carry up my bones" — to Canaan; the carry up my bones; the to Canaan; the rest there; the the land; the his bones. The expositors note the oath honoured: the oath now honoured; the oath honoured; the the event. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the God will surely visit you and ye shall carry up my bones away hence with you, and the oath kept. The verse thus records the keeping of Joseph's charge — teaching that Moses honoured the oath, the faith of the patriarch confirmed by the event.
20:
"And they took their journey from Succoth, and encamped in Etham, in the edge of the wilderness." The witnesses note the journey from Succoth to Etham, at the edge of the wilderness. Adam Clarke notes they encamped in Etham, at the edge of the wilderness, God not leading them the near way. The witnesses note Israel journeying from Succoth and encamping in Etham, at the edge of the wilderness; the orderly stages of the march under God's leading — that the journey proceeds by appointed stages: "they took their journey from Succoth, and encamped in Etham, in the edge of the wilderness"; the witnesses note the progress, "they took their journey from Succoth" — on the second day of their going out, removing from their first resting-place; the witnesses note the next station, "encamped in Etham, in the edge of the wilderness" — at the border of the great wilderness into which God was leading them, the cultivated land now behind, the desert before; the witnesses note that the Scripture records the stages of the march, that the leading of God might be traced step by step; the witnesses note that God brought them by sure stages, encamping where he appointed, the cloud going before; the witnesses note the comfort, that God orders every stage of his people's pilgrimage, none left to chance; so that Israel encamps at Etham, on the edge of the wilderness; in the edge of the wilderness. The witnesses note And they took their journey from Succoth. The witnesses note and encamped in Etham, in the edge of the wilderness.

The witnesses note Israel journeying by appointed stages from Succoth to Etham, at the edge of the wilderness — the cultivated land behind, the desert before; God ordering every stage of his people's pilgrimage, none left to chance, the cloud going before.

So the witnesses make the verse the orderly stages of the march. The lesson the expositors draw is that Israel journeyed by appointed stages to the edge of the wilderness, God ordering every stage of the pilgrimage. The witnesses note the from Succoth: "they took their journey from Succoth" — the second day; the from Succoth; the the second day; the removing; the the first rest; the on. The expositors observe the encamped in Etham: "encamped in Etham" — the next station; the encamped in Etham; the the next station; the the border; the the camp; the appointed. The witnesses press the edge of the wilderness: "in the edge of the wilderness" — the desert before; the edge of the wilderness; the the desert; the before; the the border; the the land behind. The expositors draw the lesson on the the stages: the stages of the march recorded; the the stages; the the stages; the the march; the recorded; the traced. The witnesses observe the by sure stages: God brought them by sure stages; the by sure stages; the sure stages; the appointed; the the cloud; the before. 21:
"And the LORD went before them by day in a pillar of a cloud, to lead them the way; and by night in a pillar of fire, to give them light; to go by day and night:" The witnesses note the LORD going before them in the pillar of cloud by day and fire by night. Gill notes the LORD who went before them is called the Angel of the Lord, not a created but the uncreated Angel, the Angel of Jehovah's presence, the Word and Son of God, the Lord Christ. The witnesses note the LORD going before Israel in a pillar of cloud by day to lead, and a pillar of fire by night to give light, that they might go by day and night; the gracious presence and guidance of God, leading and lighting his people — that God himself goes before them in the cloud and the fire: "the LORD went before them by day in a pillar of a cloud, to lead them the way; and by night in a pillar of fire, to give them light"; the witnesses note who it was that went before, that "the LORD" here is "the Angel of the Lord," not a created angel but "the uncreated Angel, the Angel of Jehovah's presence, the Word and Son of God, the Lord Christ," a pre-incarnate presence of Christ leading his people; the witnesses note the double mercy, "a pillar of a cloud, to lead them the way" by day (and to shade them from the heat), and "a pillar of fire, to give them light" by night (and to warm and guard them); the witnesses note the constancy, "to go by day and night" — that God's guidance was unbroken, fitted to every hour; the witnesses note the great comfort, that God himself, in Christ, leads and lights his pilgrim people through the wilderness; so that the LORD leads them in cloud and fire, day and night; to go by day and night. The witnesses note the LORD went before them by day in a pillar of a cloud, to lead them the way. The witnesses note and by night in a pillar of fire, to give them light; to go by day and night.

The witnesses note the LORD — the uncreated Angel, the Word and Son of God, a pre-incarnate presence of Christ — going before Israel in cloud by day and fire by night, to lead and to light them; the great comfort that God himself, in Christ, leads his pilgrim people through the wilderness.

So the witnesses make the verse the gracious presence and guidance of God. The lesson the expositors draw is that the LORD, the uncreated Angel, went before Israel in cloud and fire, leading and lighting them day and night, God himself in Christ guiding his pilgrim people. The witnesses note the the LORD went before: "the LORD went before them" — Gill notes the uncreated Angel; the the LORD went before; the the Word; the Christ. The expositors observe the pillar of cloud: "a pillar of a cloud, to lead them" — by day; the pillar of cloud; the to lead; the shade. The witnesses press the pillar of fire: "a pillar of fire, to give them light" — by night; the pillar of fire; the warm; the guard. The expositors draw the lesson on the day and night: "to go by day and night" — unbroken guidance; the day and night; the every hour. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and by night in a pillar of fire to give them light to go by day and night, and the guidance. The verse thus records the gracious presence of God — teaching that the LORD, the uncreated Angel, went before Israel in cloud and fire, leading and lighting them.
22:
"He took not away the pillar of the cloud by day, nor the pillar of fire by night, from before the people." The witnesses note that the pillar of cloud and fire was never taken away from before the people. Gill notes the pillar always appeared, the cloud by day a guide and shelter, the fire by night, continuing till they came through the wilderness to the borders of the land. The witnesses note that God took not away the pillar of cloud by day nor of fire by night from before the people; the unfailing constancy of God's presence and guidance — that God's leading never failed his people: "He took not away the pillar of the cloud by day, nor the pillar of fire by night, from before the people"; the witnesses note the constancy, "He took not away the pillar of the cloud by day, nor the pillar of fire by night" — that the cloud and fire were never withdrawn, the cloud always appearing by day as guide and shelter, the fire by night as light and guard; the witnesses note the duration, that this continued all the way "till they came through the wilderness to the borders of the land," the whole long journey; the witnesses note the great comfort the commentators draw, that God's presence with his people, once given, was not taken away, but abode constantly with them through all their wanderings; the witnesses note the faithfulness of God, who, having undertaken to lead his people, never forsook them, leading them all the way; the witnesses note the type of God's abiding presence with his church, never withdrawn; so that the pillar abides constantly before the people; from before the people. The witnesses note He took not away the pillar of the cloud by day, nor the pillar of fire by night. The witnesses note from before the people.

The witnesses note that the pillar of cloud and fire was never taken away from before the people — abiding constantly through the whole long journey to the borders of the land; the faithfulness of God, who, having undertaken to lead his people, never forsook them, a type of his abiding presence with his church.

So the witnesses make the verse the unfailing constancy of God's presence. The lesson the expositors draw is that the cloud and fire were never withdrawn but abode before the people through all their wanderings, the faithfulness of God who never forsakes his own.
